markdown.markdown:
Before we continue to learn about advanced production ML topics, we need to take a step back and understand the flow of data. It's very important that we have a way to produce high quality data and to do so in a reproducible and scalable manner. In this lesson, we're going to learn about the fundamentals of data engineering and how to construct a modern data stack that can scale and provide high quality data for our applications.

- Structured vs un-structured data
	- Structed data
		- neat
		- schema is known
		- data can be fit into a fixed field in a table
	- Unstrucuted data
		- No schema
		- No structure


Let's talk about the most common way of storing data a database. A database is a location where we store structed data. This location is designed in way that allows to capture and record data using crud (create, read, update, delete) operations using  [online transaction processing](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/data-guide/relational-data/online-transaction-processing) (OLTP) system. Most of the time a database records and stores data from a single applications. Often it is **not** used for the purpose of analytics or storing data accross resources or multiple applications.

The data in a database is highly detailed and we often acces the data on a non aggregated row level. The data in a database is organized and often adhears to a schema. This can eather be a relational schema (tables with rows and columns) often referred to as a SQL database. In here columns include attributes and rows indicate an object or entity. A non-relational/document datbase (key and value pairs or graph) often reffered to as a NoSQL database.

What are the popular databases?
- [MySQL](https://www.mysql.com)
- [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org)
- [MongoDB](https://www.mongodb.com)
- [Redis](https://redis.io) 



## Data warehouse

- What is a data warehouse (DWH)?
	- Database used for (online) analitycal processing (OLAP)
	- Step-up up from a database and lives "on top" of existing databases used for business intelligence. Database consumes data from all kinds of databases and creates a layer that is optimized to preform data anlytics.
	- Schema done on import
	- Storage location for data accumlated from a wide range of sources using ETL processes. Data in data warehouse is updated when the ETL runs.
		- Several applications
	- Used for downstream analytics and data science
	- Often performing operations across aggregating column values rather than accessing specific rows.
	- Shareing data across team or department soiled databases.
	- Rigid schema plan ahead before putting your data into a datawarehouse.

- What are the popular data warehouses?
	- [Amazon Redshift](https://aws.amazon.com/redshift/)
	- [Azure Synapse](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/synapse-analytics/)
	- [Google BigQuery](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/)
	- [Snowflake](https://www.snowflake.com/)
	- [Apache Hive](https://hive.apache.org)

- Differences Database vs Datawarehouse
	- Database designed for transactions, Data warehouse desinged for analytics and reporting
	- Databases are up to date and detailed, data warehouses is updated prediocally and is summarized.
	- Databases work slowly for querying large amounts of data and can slow down transactional processes, datawarehouses don't interfecer with any processes and are generally faster.

## Data lake

- What is a data lake?
	- Large storage repository that holds large amounts of raw data in its original format until you need it.
	- structure, semi-structured or un-structured
	- More flexible than DWH.
	- Often used for AI/ML in its current state or for analytics with processing.
	- Can be organized and put into Database or Data warehouse.
- What are popular datalakes solutions?
	- [Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
	- [Azure Blob Storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
	- [Google Cloud Storage](https://cloud.google.com/storage)

## Intuation Example

Shed in backyard houses:

- Tools (data) in toolboxs (database)
- Tool shed (data warehouse) where toolbox is stored 
	- Lots of toolboxes in your shed (yours, friends)
		-  We are storing their tools, however they keep them organized
- Some friends dont store their tools in toolboxes, they just dumped them (data lake) in there (shed).
- In a data lake, the data is raw and unorganized, likely unstructured. Any raw data from the data lake that hasn’t been organized into shelves (databases) or an organized system (data warehouses) is barely even a tool—in raw form, that data isn’t useful.


## Research info

- Database and data warehouses can only store data that has been structured.  A data lake, on the other hand, does not respect data like a data warehouse and a database. It stores all types of data: structured, semi-structured, or unstructured.
- **Processing**: Before data can be loaded into a data warehouse, it must have some shape and structure—in other words, a model. The process of giving data some shape and structure is called schema-on-write. A database also uses the schema-on-write approach. A data lake, on the other hand, accepts data in its raw form. When you do need to use data, you have to give it shape and structure. This is called schema-on-read, a very different way of processing data.
- **Cost**: One of most attractive features of big data technologies is the cost of storing data. Storing data with big data technologies is relatively cheaper than storing data in a data warehouse. This is because data technologies are often open source, so the licensing and community support is free. The data technologies are designed to be installed on low-cost commodity hardware. Storing a data warehouse can be costly, especially if the volume of data is large. A data lake, on the other hand, is designed for low-cost storage. A database has flexible storage costs which can either be high or low depending on the needs.
